coredata.dc:title: Tunable multicolor afterglow including white light emission from poly(acrylic acid)-based room temperature phosphorescence materials via phosphorescence FRET
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Liuqi
Zhu, Yan
Sun, Shaochen
Li, Hongye
Wu, Jiarui
Tao, Farong
Wang, Liping
Li, Guang
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with multicolor afterglow emission have important application potential in multicolor display and anti-counterfeiting. However, the preparation of polymer-based multicolor RTP materials still faces challenges. In this work, we prepare a kind of ultralong RTP materials by facilely doping phthalimide (Pth) into poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) matrices based on the hydrogen-bonding interaction. Attributed to the wide phosphorescence emission band of PAA-Pth, the ternary doping systems are constructed by using PAA-Pth as donors and commercially available fluorescent dyes rhodamine B (RhB) or fluorescein (FL) as acceptors, which display multicolor afterglow from cyan to red and green through efficient phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). More impressively, the quaternary doping systems PAA-Pth-RhB-FL with different molar ratios of FL exhibit tunable multicolor afterglow, including nearly white afterglow emission. Due to the different FRET efficiency of PAA-based multicolor RTP materials at different excitation wavelengths, they are successfully applied in color information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Energy transfer
Multicolor afterglow
White light emission
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PAA-Pth-RhB, PAA-Pth-FL and PAA-Pth-RhB-FL
Photophysical properties of PAA-Pth composites under ambient conditions
Theoretical calculation of acetic acid and Pth
Modulation of white light emission by phosphorescence FRET
Application of PAA-based multicolor RTP materials

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51503094 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2021ME149 and ZR2021ME126 ), the Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Shandong Province (Grant No. S202110447085 ), and the Research Foundation of Liaocheng University (Grant No. 318011905 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Excitation-phosphorescence spectra of PAA-Pth with excitation wavelengths from 250 to 370 nm under ambient conditions (delay time: 60 ms). (b) Phosphorescence spectra of PAA and PAA-Pth under 295 nm excitation (delay time: 60 ms). (c) Schematic illustration of the hydrogen-bonding interactions in PAA-Pth.

(a) Phosphorescence spectra of PAA-Pth with various molar ratios of AA/Pth (λex = 295 nm, delay time: 60 ms). (b) Time-resolved decay curves of PAA-Pth with various molar ratios of AA/Pth (λex = 295 nm). (c) Photographs of PAA-Pth under 302 nm UV light and after ceasing irradiation.
The energy gaps of coupled acetic acid (a), coupled Pth (b) and acetic acid-Pth (c).

(a) Simplified Jablonski diagram to illustrate the phosphorescence FRET process. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of FL and RhB in ethanol and phosphorescence spectrum of PAA-Pth. [FL] = 3 × 10−4 M, [RhB] = 10−5 M. (c) Schematic diagram of color-tunable PAA-based RTP materials by doping strategy.

(a) Phosphorescence spectra of PAA-Pth-RhB with different contents of RhB (λex = 295 nm, delay time: 60 ms). (b) Photographs of PAA-Pth-RhB with different contents of RhB under 302 nm UV light and after ceasing irradiation. (c) CIE coordinates of afterglow emissions of PAA-Pth-RhB with different contents of RhB. (d) Phosphorescence spectra of PAA-Pth-FL with different contents of FL (λex = 295 nm, delay time: 60 ms). (e) Photographs of PAA-Pth- FL with different contents of FL under 302 nm UV light and after ceasing irradiation. (f) CIE coordinates of afterglow emissions of PAA-Pth-FL with different contents of FL.

(a) Phosphorescence spectra of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL with different ratios of FL (λex = 295 nm, delay time: 60 ms). (b) Photographs of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL under 302 nm UV light and after ceasing irradiation. (c) CIE coordinates of afterglow emissions of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL.

(a) “000” pattern designed with PAA-RhB, PAA-Pth-RhB, PAA-FL and PAA-Pth-FL. (b) Demonstration of information encryption of the “000” pattern under 302 and 365 nm UV light and after ceasing irradiation.

head.ce:title: Tunable multicolor afterglow including white light emission from poly(acrylic acid)-based room temperature phosphorescence materials via phosphorescence FRET

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with multicolor afterglow emission have important application potential in multicolor display and anti-counterfeiting. However, the preparation of polymer-based multicolor RTP materials still faces challenges. In this work, we prepare a kind of ultralong RTP materials by facilely doping phthalimide (Pth) into poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) matrices based on the hydrogen-bonding interaction. Attributed to the wide phosphorescence emission band of PAA-Pth, the ternary doping systems are constructed by using PAA-Pth as donors and commercially available fluorescent dyes rhodamine B (RhB) or fluorescein (FL) as acceptors, which display multicolor afterglow from cyan to red and green through efficient phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). More impressively, the quaternary doping systems PAA-Pth-RhB-FL with different molar ratios of FL exhibit tunable multicolor afterglow, including nearly white afterglow emission. Due to the different FRET efficiency of PAA-based multicolor RTP materials at different excitation wavelengths, they are successfully applied in color information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted widespread attention due to their potential applications in many fields, such as organic light-emitting diodes, background-free biological imaging, chemical sensors, data encryption and anti-counterfeiting [1–5]. Most of the RTP materials are constructed by assist of noble metals or rare-earth elements with high spin-orbit coupling (SOC), which is beneficial to facilitate the efficiency of intersystem crossing (ISC) process and the radiative decay of triplet excited states [6,7]. Nevertheless, high cost and severe toxicity of noble metals and rare-earth elements greatly hinder the wide-scale commercial application of these RTP materials. In recent years, organic RTP materials have been widely investigated due to their low cost, large Stokes shift, excellent biocompatibility and facile preparation [8–10]. Though most of organic small molecule-based RTP materials have been prepared by crystal engineering, the disadvantages of poor structural design flexibility and difficult processing of crystalline organic compounds hinder the application of such organic RTP materials. Polymers can be easily modified and processed, and polymer-based RTP systems can suppress phosphor motion and promote ISC from the lowest excited singlet state (S1) to the triplet state (Tn) through multiple intramolecular and intermolecular interactions, such as covalent bonding, ionic bonding, halogen bonding and hydrogen bonding [11–18]. Therefore, the development of polymer-based RTP materials has broad prospects.

A series of strategies including doping [19–25], copolymerization [26–30], supramolecular assembly [31–33], host-guest system [34–36], and carbon dots [37–39], have been applied to construct polymer systems to achieve RTP. Polymer-based RTP materials mainly rely on the effective intermolecular electrostatic interactions between polymer matrix and phosphors to improve the ISC efficiency, which contributes to achieve effective phosphorescence emission. Due to containing abundant carboxyl functional groups, PAA is a promising candidate for the construction of polymer-based RTP materials. The existence of a large number of carbonyl groups and oxygen atoms in PAA is beneficial to enhance the SOC, which is an important factor to facilitate ISC and realize phosphorescence emission [40]. Besides, carboxyl groups in PAA can easily form hydrogen bonding networks with the doped phosphors, which have an efficient shielding effect on intermolecular collision deactivation and inhibit phosphorescence quenching. Nevertheless, PAA-based RTP materials are still rarely reported.

Multicolor emission properties facilitate the higher-order applications of RTP materials in multicolor display, multilevel information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and polychromatic imaging [41–44]. Due to the characteristics of special structure and easy modification, polymers show great advantages in preparing multicolor RTP materials. Zhao and co-workers synthesized PAA-based RTP materials through radical multicomponent cross-linked copolymerization, which displayed multicolor luminescence by changing the excitation wavelength from 254 to 370 nm [26]. Li and co-workers developed a series of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-based RTP materials with full-color tunability by the modification of PVA with arylboronic acids [45]. Ma and co-workers synthesized three multicolor RTP copolymers by copolymerizing benzoic acid, 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride and acrylamide, which could give rise to tunable multicolor RTP including nearly white afterglow through the changes of feeding ratios and excitation wavelengths [27]. Recently, the phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy through transferring triplet (T1) excitons of donor (host) to singlet (S1) excitons of acceptor (guest) has been developed to prepare multicolor RTP materials [46–49]. Liu and co-workers realized multicolor RTP based on the phosphorescence FRET by doping organic dyes Eosin Y or SR101 into polyacrylamide-based supramolecular RTP materials [46]. Chen and co-workers developed full-color organic afterglow materials by doping different guests into a polyacrylamide-based RTP matrix via phosphorescence FRET, which could generate varied afterglow color ranging from blue to red and even white [47]. Compared with multicomponent copolymerization and modification strategies, the doping strategy combined with phosphorescence FRET is more convenient to construct polymer-based multicolor RTP materials. However, to the best of our knowledge, the reports about the acceptor emission through phosphorescence FRET of the donor are rare, and the development of polymer-based multicolor RTP materials still remains a significant challenge.

Herein, we prepare a class of ultralong PAA-based RTP materials (PAA-Pth) by doping phthalimide (Pth) into PAA matrices, which show bright cyan afterglows with the phosphorescence lifetime of up to 287 ms. To our unexpected, tunable multicolor afterglows via phosphorescence FRET are realized through the introduction of rhodamine B (RhB) or fluorescein (FL) into the PAA-Pth matrices, and the afterglow colors range from cyan to yellow, green and even red. Inspired by the principle of modulating white light emission, a nearly white afterglow is successfully achieved by doping suitable contents of RhB and FL into the PAA-Pth matrix. Furthermore, the phosphorescence FRET PAA-based multicolor RTP materials can be applied in the field of information encryption.

Acrylic acid (AA) was purchased from Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Phthalimid (Pth) and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (China), and AIBN was recrystallized from ethanol. Rhodamine B (RhB) and fluorescein (FL) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout the whole experiment process. All other chemicals were commercially available and used as received.

The relative number-average molecular weight (M
n ) and molecular weight distribution (MWD) of PAA were obtained from an aqueous gel permeation chromatography (Breeze, Waters, USA) using 0.1 mol/L NaNO3 aqueous solution (0.8 mL/min) as eluent and polysaccharide as the calibration standard at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from a Bruker Vector-22 IR spectrometer using KBr pellets at room temperature. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The quantum yields were determined on an Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere at room temperature. Emission spectra and lifetimes were also measured on the Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. Steady state photoluminescence spectra were determined after excitation using a xenon lamp. Phosphorescence spectra were obtained with a delay time of 60 ms after excitation using a microsecond flash lamp. The lifetimes of the luminescence were determined from the fitting function of I(t) = A
1
e
-t/τ1 + A
2
e
-t/τ2 + A
3
e
-t/τ3 according to the time-resolved emission decay curves, where A and τ represented the amplitudes and lifetimes of the individual components for multi-exponential decay profiles, respectively.

ORCA 4.2.1 program was used to perform geometry optimizations, and the functional PBE0 and the TZVP basis set were used for all geometry optimizations. ORCA 4.2.1 was further applied to perform time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations. The optimized stationary point was confirmed by harmonic vibrational frequency analysis to ensure that real local minima were reached. The excitation energies of the singlets (Sn) and triplets (Tn) as well as SOC matrix elements between the singlet excited states and triplet excited states were calculated at the level of PBE0/def2-TZVP by ORCA 4.2.1 program.

In a 250 mL three-necked round bottom flask, AA (10.5 g) and AIBN (52.5 mg) were added dropwise to a stirred mixed solution of n-hexane (46 mL) and n-dodecyl mercaptan (0.9 mL) at 80 °C within 0.5 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was then further stirred at 80 °C for 4 h under nitrogen atmosphere, during which white solids were precipitated from the solution. After filtration, the white powders were dried under vacuum at room temperature for 24 h. And then, the white solids were dissolved in deionized water and dialyzed using the regenerated cellulose membrane tube with a cut-off molecular weight of 1000 for 3 days. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain white powder product PAA (8.1 g, M
n = 20,700, MWD = 2.75).

PAA (50 mg) and Pth (0.1 mg) were dissolved in 4 mL deionized water. After stirring for 5 min, the mixed solution was dried in the oven at 120 °C for 5 h to obtain white solid PAA-Pth with 1000:1 M ratio of AA to Pth. PAA-Pth with other molar ratios of AA to Pth were prepared in the same way, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PAA-Pth-RhB, PAA-Pth-FL and PAA-Pth-RhB-FL
ce:section.ce:para: PAA-Pth-RhB, PAA-Pth-FL and PAA-Pth-RhB-FL with different molar ratios of AA to the doped small molecules were prepared following the similar procedure as the preparation of PAA-Pth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of PAA-Pth composites under ambient conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Abundant carboxyl groups in PAA are conducive to form a rigid microenvironment through hydrogen-bonding interactions with doped guest molecules, and PAA as matrix to immobilize the chromophore shows advantages in preparing RTP materials [50]. Pth has carbonyl group and amino group, which can easily form hydrogen bond with electron-donating group and electron-absorbing group respectively. Furthermore, the excellent planarity of Pth contributes to more efficient intramolecular charge transfer, which facilitates the mobility of conjugated electrons [51]. And so, Pth was selected as the guest phosphor to dope PAA to construct PAA-based RTP materials. Just as we expected, after ceasing the irradiation of 302 nm UV light, PAA-Pth with AA/Pth molar ratio of 1000:1 displayed a long-lived and strong cyan afterglow under ambient conditions, and no naked-eye recognizable afterglow was observed from PAA. As illustrated in Fig. S1, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of PAA-Pth exhibits the emission peak at 331 nm, while phosphorescence spectrum of PAA-Pth shows a significant emission peak at 460 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength for PAA-Pth is determined to be 295 nm from the excitation-phosphorescence spectra (Fig. 1 a). As shown in Fig. 1b, PAA shows an extremely weak phosphorescence emission, while the Pth-doped system PAA-Pth exhibits a highly strong phosphorescence emission peak at 460 nm under ambient conditions. Compared with only 0.792 ms phosphorescence lifetime of PAA, PAA-Pth with AA/Pth molar ratio of 1000:1 has an ultralong lifetime, which is up to 287 ms (Fig. S2). Obviously, Pth plays an important role in the enhancement of phosphorescence intensity and phosphorescence lifetime of the PAA-Pth system. The carbonyl group and amino group of Pth can form the cross-linked hydrogen bond network with hydroxyl group and carbonyl group of PAA respectively (Fig. 1c). To prove the intermolecular hydrogen-bonding interactions formed between PAA and Pth, FTIR spectra of PAA and PAA-Pth with AA/Pth molar ratio of 1000:1 were measured (Fig. S3). PAA shows an obvious peak at 3421 cm−1, which can be attributed to the intramolecular hydrogen bonds of PAA. When Pth is doped into PAA matrix, the peak shifts to the lower wavenumber of 3415 cm−1, and the peak shape becomes broader, indicating the formation of intermolecular hydrogen-bonding interactions between PAA and Pth. Therefore, it can be concluded that the hydrogen-bonding interactions facilitate the RTP emission of PAA-Pth under ambient conditions.

The doping amount of the phosphors may affect the phosphorescence properties of the doped RTP materials. And so, the effect of AA/Pth molar ratio was systematically investigated by preparing a series of PAA-Pth with AA/Pth molar ratios from 1000:0.1 to 1000:30 (Fig. 2 ). PAA-Pth with AA/Pth molar ratio of 1000:1 exhibits the strongest phosphorescence intensity and an ultrolong phosphorescence lifetime of 287 ms. After ceasing the irradiation of 302 nm UV light, PAA-Pth with AA/Pth molar ratio of 1000:1 also displays the longest naked-eye recognizable cyan afterglow in a series of PAA-Pth systems with different AA/Pth molar ratios. Furthermore, PAA-Pth has a decent phosphorescence quantum yield of 8.87% in 1000:1 M ratio of AA to Pth. The phosphorescence intensities and lifetimes of PAA-Pth are all reduced when the molar ratio of AA to Pth is low or high (Fig. S4). In the lower content of Pth, few hydrogen bonds are formed between Pth and PAA matrix, while Pth in higher content may form the aggregated states and cannot all form hydrogen bonds with PAA [52]. Because the low content of hydrogen bonds between Pth and PAA matrix might not efficiently suppress non-radiative transition of the triplet state, PAA-Pth systems with low or high concentration of Pth cannot show excellent RTP properties. Therefore, the optimal doping molar ratio of AA to Pth is 1000:1 in the PAA-Pth systems, and the appropriate doping ratio of phosphors is beneficial to the enhancement of phosphorescence performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation of acetic acid and Pth
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper insight into the RTP performance of PAA-Pth, theoretical calculations were carried out. According to Franck-Condon principle, small energy gaps (ΔEST) and large SOC constants from singlet states to triplet states facilitate the ISC process [6]. And so, acetic acid and Pth were used as models to calculate SOC constants and ΔEST. As shown in Table S1 and Fig. 3 , the SOC constants and ΔEST of coupled acetic acid, coupled Pth and acetic acid-Pth in the gas state are calculated by time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT). According to the energy gap law, there are multiple triplet states in acetic acid-Pth with ΔEST lower than 0.37 eV, meaning that acetic acid-Pth system is more conducive to ISC [53]. Compared with coupled acetic acid and coupled Pth, acetic acid-Pth has more number of efficient ISC channels with ξ(S1-Tn) > 0.3 cm−1, which also confirms the feasible ISC process in acetic acid-Pth system according to the EI-Sayed rule [54]. Furthermore, the ξ(T1-S0) of acetic acid-Pth is only 0.053 cm−1, which may reduce the radiative transition rate from T1 to S0 and extend the phosphorescence lifetime. The results indicate that the blending of acetic acid and Pth is favorable for phosphorescence emission, and further proves the highly efficient RTP performance of PAA-Pth.

The ultralong phosphorescence lifetime and wide phosphorescence emission band from 380 to 650 nm make PAA-Pth as an ideal host candidate to prepare polymer-based multicolor materials through phosphorescence FRET process, in which long-lived T1 excitons of PAA-Pth donor transfer energy to the S1 state of the acceptor via a delayed sensitization process (Fig. 4 a). As shown in Fig. 4b, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the commercial dyes rhodamine B (RhB) and fluorescein (FL) overlap well with the phosphorescence emission spectrum of PAA-Pth. Based on the prerequisite of spectral overlap required for phosphorescence FRET, RhB and FL as the acceptors were doped into PAA-Pth matrices to investigate the phosphorescence FRET processes (Fig. 4c).

Firstly, various molar ratios of RhB were doped into the PAA-Pth matrices with AA/Pth molar ratio of 1000:1 to construct ternary doping systems PAA-Pth-RhB. PAA-Pth-RhB with the AA/Pth/RhB molar ratio of 1000:1:0.3 shows the most prominent emission band under the optimum excitation wavelength of 295 nm (Fig. S5). Compared with PAA-Pth, PAA-Pth-RhB presents obviously distinct photophysical properties under ambient conditions. The spectrum of PAA-Pth has only one phosphorescence emission peak at 460 nm, while PAA-Pth-RhB shows two delayed emission peaks at 460 nm and 627 nm, respectively (Fig. S6). Considering the phosphorescence emission peak of PAA-Pth and the fluorescence emission peak of RhB, two emission peaks of PAA-Pth-RhB might come from the phosphorescence of PAA-Pth and the fluorescence of RhB. As shown in Fig. S7, an extremely weak delayed emission peak at 627 nm is observed from the phosphorescence spectrum of PAA-RhB under 295 nm excitation, and steady-state photoluminescence spectrum of PAA-RhB exhibits a strong emission peak at 627 nm, which confirms that the emission peak at 627 nm in the phosphorescence spectrum of PAA-Pth-RhB belongs to the fluorescence of RhB. The delayed lifetime of PAA-Pth-RhB with AA/Pth/RhB molar ratio of 1000:1:0.3 at 627 nm is up to 46 ms (Fig. S8), while the lifetime of PAA-RhB with AA/RhB molar ratio of 1000:0.3 at 627 nm is only 2 ms (Fig. S9). Therefore, it can be concluded that the phosphorescence FRET from PAA-Pth to RhB contributes to the emergence of RhB fluorescence with long lifetime in the delayed emission spectrum. The emission intensity of phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence in the phosphorescence FRET systems are closely related to the content of the doped acceptors. With increasing the molar ratio of RhB from 0.003 to 0.3 in PAA-Pth-RhB systems, the phosphorescence emission intensity of PAA-Pth centered at 460 nm gradually decreases, and the delayed emission intensity of RhB in the range of 560–750 nm increases accordingly (Fig. 5 a). Furthermore, a significant red shift of delayed emission peak from 591 nm to 627 nm is observed. With the increase of RhB content, RhB molecules tend to aggregate. Compared with the monomolecular state in the low concentration of RhB, the aggregated state has lower energy levels, thus causing the red shift of the emission peak [55]. Amazingly, PAA-Pth-RhB with AA/Pth/RhB molar ratio of 1000:1:0.3 shows an ultrahigh phosphorescence quantum yield of 77.1% (Fig. S10). These results prove the efficient energy transfer from triplet states of PAA-Pth to singlet states of RhB (Table S2). However, the emission intensities at 460 nm and 627 nm are both decreased with the continuous increase of RhB content. The excess of RhB might increase the probability of collision deactivation between dye molecules, which facilitate the non-radiative transition rate of phosphorescence FRET and severely weaken the persistent luminescence intensity and lifetime (Figs. S11–S14). As shown in Fig. 5b, the color of long-persistent luminescence of PAA-Pth-RhB is effectively regulated from pink to red by increasing the content of RhB. The Commission International de l’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of PAA-Pth-RhB are calculated from the afterglow emissions of PAA-Pth-RhB with different contents of RhB, which further demonstrate the afterglow color-tunability from pink to red with the increase of RhB content (Fig. 5c).

Subsequently, the acceptor was replaced with FL to investigate the phosphorescence FRET universality of ternary doping system, and a series of PAA-Pth-FL with AA/Pth/FL molar ratio from 1000:1:0.004 to 1000:1:2.222 were prepared. After doping with FL, excitation-phosphorescence mapping of PAA-Pth-FL displays a new distinct emission band centered at 526 nm (Fig. S15). Compared with almost negligible delayed emission intensity and 2.68 ms delayed lifetime of PAA-FL, PAA-Pth-FL with AA/Pth/FL molar ratio of 1000:1:0.222 exhibits strong delayed emission, and the delayed lifetime is up to 128 ms (Figs. S16–S18). As shown in Fig. S19, the delayed emission peak of PAA-Pth-FL at 526 nm is perfectly matched with steady-state PL spectrum of PAA-FL, which indicates that the emission peak at 526 nm is derived from the fluorescence of FL through the phosphorescence FRET process. Just as PAA-Pth-RhB systems, the same trends of the delayed emission properties are observed from PAA-Pth-FL systems with varied FL contents (Fig. 5d, Figs. S20–S22). With the increase of FL content, the phosphorescence intensity of PAA-Pth-FL at 460 nm decreases gradually, and the delayed emission intensity at 526 nm shows an increasing trend followed by a decreasing trend. PAA-Pth-FL with AA/Pth/FL molar ratio of 1000:1:0.222 has the strongest delayed emission intensity at 526 nm and a high phosphorescence quantum yield of 17.1% (Figs. S23 and 24). The phosphorescence energy transfer efficiency (ΦP-FRET) of PAA-Pth-FL is up to 76.04% at 0.222 M ratio of FL, meaning the highly efficient phosphorescence FRET process (Table S3). Intriguingly, with the increasing content of FL gradually, the persistent luminescence color of PAA-Pth-FL can be tuned from cyan to green and finally to yellow under ambient conditions (Fig. 5e and f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulation of white light emission by phosphorescence FRET
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent multicolor luminescence performance of PAA-based phosphorescence FRET systems prompted us to further explore white light emission. It is well known that white light materials have great application value in the display field [56–58]. However, the reports about organic white light emission from polymer-based RTP materials are still rare. White light emission requires emission peak position and emission intensity of the materials to match well [59–61]. Therefore, we attempted to construct a series of quaternary doping systems by doping FL into PAA-Pth-RhB matrices, aiming to realize the delayed white light emission through phosphorescence FRET strategy. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the delayed emission peak of PAA-Pth-RhB at 506 nm gradually become prominent with the increase of FL content under the excitation wavelength of 295 nm due to the phosphorescence FRET process. The same trend is observed from the steady-state PL spectra of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL (Fig. S25). When the molar ratio of FL increases to 0.035, the photoluminescence color of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL changes from pink to white under 302 nm UV light (Fig. 6b and Fig. S26). Impressively, the afterglow color of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL with AA/Pth/RhB/FL molar ratio of 1000:1:0.003:0.035 can be tuned from cyan to nearly white after ceasing the irradiation of 302 nm UV light, and the CIE coordinates of the delayed emission are calculated to be (0.33, 0.32), which are very close to the standard white light emission (0.33, 0.33) (Fig. 6c). The results demonstrate that the contents of FL affect the position and intensity of the delayed emission peaks and further lead to the transition of the afterglow color. Since the emission intensity might be influenced by the excitation wavelength, the delayed emission intensities of PAA-Pth-RhB-FL with FL molar ratio of 0.035 were further investigated under different excitation wavelengths. The intensities of the delayed emission peaks at about 500 nm and 600 nm both show remarkable changes under different excitation wavelength varying from 250 to 370 nm, and the near-white afterglow emission can be excited by a wide range of excitation wavelengths from 250 to 320 nm (Figs. S27 and S28).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PAA-based multicolor RTP materials
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent photophysical performances of PAA-based RTP materials through phosphorescence FRET, we explored their potential application in information encryption (Fig. 7 ). An anti-counterfeiting pattern “000” is fabricated by combining PAA-RhB, PAA-Pth-RhB, PAA-FL and PAA-Pth-FL. Under 302 and 365 nm UV light, the colorful pattern “000” is observed. However, after ceasing the UV irradiation, the pattern shows completely different information. After creasing the irradiation of 302 nm UV light, the misleading information of the colorful digital pattern “171” is displayed. The right information of the green number “7” is revealed when 365 nm UV light is turned off.

In summary, a kind of PAA-based RTP materials PAA-Pth were simply prepared by doping Pth into PAA matrices. The fabricated PAA-Pth exhibited excellent RTP properties based on the hydrogen-bonding interaction. More amazingly, by facilely doping acceptor dye molecules (RhB or FL) into PAA-Pth donor matrices, multicolor afterglows were successfully achieved through highly efficient phosphorescence FRET. The afterglow color of the ternary doping systems could vary from cyan to yellow or red with the increase of FL or RhB content. Due to the different afterglow color of RhB and FL doped systems via phosphorescence FRET, the quaternary doping systems PAA-Pth-RhB-FL were prepared, and their afterglow colors could be tuned from cyan to yellow including nearly white by changing FL content and excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the phosphorescence FRET PAA-based multicolor RTP materials showed promising application potential in color information encryption. This work provides a guideline for the construction of polymer-based RTP materials using the simple preparation procedure and commercially available raw materials to achieve multicolor afterglow and even white light emission via phosphorescence FRET.

Liuqi Kong: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yan Zhu: Investigation, Data curation. Shaochen Sun: Validation, Formal analysis. Hongye Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Jiarui Wu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Farong Tao: Validation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Liping Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Guang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51503094), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2021ME149 and ZR2021ME126), the Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Shandong Province (Grant No. S202110447085), and the Research Foundation of Liaocheng University (Grant No. 318011905).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111242.
